hook: Noon procession of automaton apostles inside the cathedral, with a crowing cock
facts: Current mechanism dates from 1843, the third clock on the spot since 1352
At solar noon (12:30) carved apostles parade past Christ while a life-size cock beats its wings and crows thrice
The four ages of life file past Death, who strikes the hour with a bone
The original 1354 clock's gilt rooster, the oldest automaton in the world, survives in the Palais Rohan
legend: They say the builder Schwilgue was blinded so he could never make a second clock; in fact he died in his bed
w: Wait in the south transept for 12:30 and the mechanical theatre cranks awake: apostles shuffling past a blessing Christ, a gilded cock beating its wings and crowing three times, the four ages of man filing past a skeleton who taps out the hour with a bone. Built 1843. Time, performed.

n: The Pillar of Angels and the Devil's Window
hook: A slender 13th-century pillar a peasant supposedly waited centuries to see collapse
facts: The Pillar of Angels (Pilier des Anges), carved around 1230, holds the Last Judgement in three tiers of figures
A small stone man leans from a balustrade nearby, traditionally the doubter watching for the pillar to fall
The wind that whips around Place du Chateau feeds the cathedral's most repeated devil legend
Strasbourg Cathedral was the world's tallest building from 1647 to 1874
legend: Legend says a sceptic swore the thin pillar would crumble and stood watching for years; masons carved him in stone to keep his vigil forever
They say the Devil rode the wind in to admire his own portrait on the facade, got trapped inside, and the wind still howls outside waiting for him
w: A single 13th-century pillar carries the whole Last Judgement in carved tiers. Legend says a doubter swore the thin column would topple and stood watching for years; masons fixed him in stone, leaning out to watch it stand. They say the wind outside still howls because the Devil's trapped within.

hook: A cheese merchant's 1589 house drowning in carved oak and bottle-glass windows
facts: Stone ground floor dates to 1467; the corbelled timber upper floors were added in 1589 by cheese merchant Martin Braun
Seventy-five bullseye-glass windows glaze the facade
Carvings run through sacred and profane themes, the corner posts showing Faith, Hope and Charity
Now a restaurant, listed as a historic monument
w: Stand under it and count the carvings: musicians, zodiac signs, virgins wise and foolish, all swarming across timber a Renaissance cheese merchant paid for in 1589 to advertise his taste. Seventy-five bullseye windows bulge over the square. The stone floor below is older still. Vanity in oak.

hook: A 1690 defensive weir whose roof terrace surveys the towers, lined with cathedral casts
facts: Built 1686-1700 to a design after Vauban, it could dam the Ill to flood attackers' approach
An enclosed gallery houses plaster casts and copies of statues and gargoyles from the cathedral and Palais Rohan
The panoramic roof terrace, added 1965-66, looks over the Ponts Couverts and Petite France
Free to enter
w: Vauban's engineers could drown an attacking army here, raising the Ill to flood the southern approach. Today you climb past a cold gallery of plaster gargoyles and saints, cast off the cathedral, to a free rooftop terrace over the covered bridges. A weapon of water, repurposed as a viewpoint.

hook: Three squat 13th-century river towers that were the city's prisons for six centuries
facts: Part of a defensive line begun around 1230; the bridges lost their wooden roofs in the 18th century but kept the name
Four towers stood guard, three survive (the Malzenturm was razed in 1869)
They served as prisons until 1823; the Tour Henri preserves timbers dated to 1229
Prisoners left some 500 graffiti motifs and dates carved between 1530 and 1595
w: The 'covered' bridges lost their roofs centuries ago but the name stuck. The real story sits in the three blunt towers: medieval prisons until 1823, their cells scratched with five hundred prisoners' marks from the 1500s. Timbers in the Tour Henri were felled in 1229. Stone with a long memory.

hook: Medieval cellar under the hospital holding the world's oldest wine still aged in a barrel
facts: The vaulted cellar was dug beneath the civil hospital in 1395
A foudre holds white wine from the 1472 vintage, the oldest barrel-aged wine in the world
It has been tasted only three times: 1576, 1716 and 1944 (for the Liberation of Strasbourg)
Patients once paid for care in wine, which the hospice cellared and sold
w: Patients once settled their hospital bills in wine, and the surplus went down here in 1395. One barrel still holds a white from 1472, the oldest wine aging in wood anywhere. It has been opened three times in five centuries: for a Swiss delegation, after a fire, and for Strasbourg's liberation in 1944. They still sell bottles upstairs.

hook: Alsace's Protestant 'cathedral', with a marshal's theatrical tomb and Mozart's organ
facts: Main Lutheran church of the city since the cathedral reverted to Catholic worship in 1681
Pigalle's 1777 mausoleum of Marshal Maurice de Saxe stages the marshal striding toward an open tomb as Death lifts the lid
The 1741 Silbermann organ was played by Mozart in 1778
Albert Schweitzer played and championed the church's organs
w: The cathedral went Catholic in 1681, so the Lutherans made this their own. Inside, Pigalle carved Marshal de Saxe descending into a tomb while a skeletal Death holds the lid and France weeps. The 1741 Silbermann organ that Mozart played in 1778 still sounds; Schweitzer fought to save it. Plain walls, loud ghosts.

hook: A Protestant church wrapped around an 11th-century cloister with 80 old tombstones
facts: Built from 1031 on a Merovingian chapel; three of the four cloister galleries are 11th-century Romanesque
The cloister holds around 80 tombstones, mainly 14th- to 16th-century
Frescoes survive from the 14th century onward, with a rare choir screen unique in Alsace
Lutheran since 1524
w: Pass through the working church to find the real prize behind it: a Romanesque cloister whose arches were raised in the 11th century, paving worn by eight hundred years and walled with eighty medieval tombstones. Faded frescoes, a choir screen found nowhere else in Alsace. Quiet, and few people know to look.

hook: Prince-bishops' baroque palace, scene of the necklace scandal and home to the clock's old cock
facts: Built 1732-42 for the Rohan prince-bishops to designs by Robert de Cotte
Cardinal de Rohan was disgraced here in the 1785 Affair of the Diamond Necklace that helped sink Marie Antoinette's name
Houses three museums; the original gilt rooster of the 1354 astronomical clock is among the decorative arts
Hosted Louis XV, Marie Antoinette and Napoleon
w: The Rohan prince-bishops built this riverside palace in the 1730s to outshine the cathedral next door. Inside these rooms Cardinal de Rohan was duped in the diamond-necklace swindle that helped wreck Marie Antoinette's reputation. The decorative-arts museum guards the cathedral clock's 1354 rooster, oldest automaton on earth.

hook: Folk life of old Alsace inside a warren of timber houses linked by creaking galleries
facts: Opened in 1907 across several Renaissance timber-framed houses on the Quai Saint-Nicolas
Over 5,000 objects of rural and pre-industrial Alsatian daily life, including reconstructed interiors
Visitors climb wooden stairs and cross open galleries between merged old houses
Closed for renovation, due to reopen in 2028
w: Three old riverside houses knocked together in 1907 to save a vanishing world: painted bedsteads, kitchen gods, winemakers' tools, a whole Jewish village room. You wander up crooked stairs and across wooden balconies, never sure which house you're in. Folk Alsace, before the factories. Shut for works until 2028.

hook: An 1884 villa stuffed with a local provocateur's children's books, satire and erotica
facts: Holds 14,000 drawings donated by Strasbourg-born illustrator Tomi Ungerer (1931-2019)
Set in the Villa Greiner, an 1884 mansion in the Neustadt
Galleries run from beloved children's books to fierce anti-war and advertising satire to an explicit erotic room
Ungerer was both a Caldecott-winning author and a banned cartoonist
w: Tomi Ungerer drew tender children's books, savage anti-Vietnam posters and graphic erotica, often the same week, which got him quietly blacklisted for decades. His home town took the lot, 14,000 drawings, and hung them in an 1884 villa. Climb the stairs and the picture books give way to the unprintable. One restless hand.

hook: A jeering crowd's 'Sistine Chapel of abstract art' hidden behind an 18th-century facade
facts: In 1926-28 Theo van Doesburg, Jean Arp and Sophie Taeuber-Arp built a De Stijl leisure complex inside an old guardhouse on Place Kleber
Diagonal coloured planes meant to place the visitor inside the painting
The 1928 public hated it and the rooms were soon altered, then forgotten
Restored and reopened in 2006; entry is free
w: Behind a staid 18th-century front, three artists turned a cinema-ballroom into pure De Stijl in 1928, tilting colour planes to drop the dancer inside the painting. Strasbourg recoiled, the owners painted over it, and it slept for decades. Now called the Sistine Chapel of abstract art, restored, free to enter. The mob lost.

hook: A concrete postwar synagogue raised because the Nazis blew the old one up
facts: Inaugurated 1958 to replace the grand quai Kleber synagogue, looted and burned by the Nazis in September 1940 then dynamited
Designed by Claude Meyer-Levy on a concrete basilica plan beside the Parc du Contades
Its facade carries a wave of Stars of David and it seats around 1,600
Named 'of Peace' in deliberate answer to the destruction
w: In September 1940 the Nazis looted, burned and then dynamited the city's great synagogue on the quai Kleber, erasing it. This blunt concrete answer rose in 1958 by the Contades park, its front rippling with Stars of David, and they named it 'of Peace'. A reply written in poured concrete to an act of demolition.

n: Storks of the Parc de l'Orangerie
hook: The park that brought Alsace's vanished white storks back from near-extinction
facts: Strasbourg's oldest park, laid out around a 1804 pavilion named for Josephine
By the 1970s wild white storks had all but disappeared from Alsace
A reintroduction centre here helped the symbol of the region recover; nests now crowd the trees and rooftops
Free to enter, with a small zoo and bowling lake
legend: Alsatian lore long held that storks nesting on your chimney bring babies and good luck
w: By the 1970s the white stork, emblem of Alsace, had nearly vanished from its skies. A breeding station in this old park, where Josephine's orangery still stands, helped bring it back. Now great clattering nests crowd the trees and roofs, birds wheeling overhead. Old lore says they bring babies; mostly they bring noise.

hook: A glass parliament whose tower was left deliberately unfinished, like a modern Babel
facts: Opened 1999 by Architecture-Studio at the meeting of the Ill and the Marne-Rhine canal
Its 60-metre elliptical tower is intentionally left looking incomplete on one side
The hemicycle seats 750-plus members, the largest of any European institution
Named after Louise Weiss, journalist, suffragist and the Parliament's oldest member in 1979
w: Architecture-Studio gave the Parliament a glass drum and a 60-metre tower left looking unfinished on purpose, an echo of Bruegel's Tower of Babel and Europe's many tongues. Critics read it as a building that admits it isn't done. Sessions run here twelve weeks a year while Brussels keeps the rest. Ambition, scaffolded.

hook: A galleried Renaissance inn that took in emperors travelling incognito for three centuries
facts: Documented as the inn 'Zum Rappen' from 1528, with a post house here earlier still
Guests over three centuries included Marshal Turenne and, travelling secretly, Emperor Joseph II and Frederick the Great
Closed as an inn in 1854; glassmakers Ott Freres then held it for some 130 years
Listed in 1930, reopened as a hotel in 2009
w: Step off the lane into a galleried timber courtyard that took in travellers from 1528. Emperors slipped in under false names here, Joseph II and Frederick the Great among them, alongside the open arrivals of dukes and marshals. After 1854 glassmakers held it, then neglect. A hotel again now, but the well and the wood are real.

hook: A 1908 imperial bathhouse with Roman pools, hammams and tiled vaults, still swimming
facts: Built 1905-08 by German architect Fritz Beblo in the late imperial style
Two pools, Roman baths, saunas and hammams under marble and stucco vaults
Classified a historic monument; carefully restored and reopened in 2021
People have swum here continuously since 1908
w: The Germans built this temple to public bathing in 1908: two pools under tiled vaults, Roman baths, hammams, stucco and marble where the city has scrubbed and swum for over a century. A careful 2021 restoration brought it back without scrubbing off the grandeur. Bring a towel and swim in a monument.
